properties.Site location: White Sands Missile Range, New Mexico
properties.label: Trinity Site
properties.Notes: A bombing range and more lately a missile range centered in the south near Las Cruces, an area in the north part of the range was acquired during World War II and used for the Trinity test. An area near the Trinity site is designated the Permanent High Explosive Test Site (PHETS) and was used in the 1980s to host very large ANFO blasts for international testing of military gear.The Trinity nuclear site was originally private property taken over by the Army to test the plutonium implosion weapon, the first nuclear explosion on Earth.

properties.label: Hiroshima, Japan
properties.Notes: The first target of nuclear weapons, the Mark I atomic bomb. The target was the Aioi Bridge across the Ōta River; it exploded several hundred yards off. Hiroshima was a city of 250,000, suffering 70,000 or so deaths immediately and up to 126,000 by the end of the year.

properties.label: Nagasaki, Japan
properties.Notes: The second target of nuclear weapons, Nagasaki was a city of 240,000 swelled to 263,000 on the day of the strike, chosen when the primary target, Kokura, was found clouded over. About 40,000 died immediately, and officially 73,884 died altogether. 74,909 were injured. Hillier than Hiroshima, the 33% increase in yield from Little Boy resulted in slightly fewer casualties.

properties.label: Bikini Atoll
properties.Notes: An atoll in the Marshall Islands, Bikini's lagoon was chosen as the site for the first nuclear test area after WWII. Its use ended with the Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty, which forbade atmospheric testing.

properties.label: Enewetak Atoll
properties.Notes: The second atoll used for nuclear testing by the US. Near Bikini Atoll, they served as each other's alternate area.

properties.label: Pacific Ocean
properties.Notes: Three US tests were performed in the open ocean away from all islands: Wigwam, Swordfish and Frigate Bird.

properties.label: Johnston Atoll
properties.Notes: A small, isolated atoll closest to Hawaii (500 km), Johnston Island served the US testing efforts as an airbase for Operation Dominic and as a rocket base for Operation Hardtack II and Operation Fishbowl.

properties.label: Kiritimati, Kiribati
properties.Notes: Formerly known as Christmas Island (the Pacific one, not the one in the Indian Ocean) Kiritimati was used as an air base for bombers dropping nuclear tests mostly south of the island. It hosted most of the Operation Dominic drops as well as most of Britain's Operation Grapple.

properties.Site location: Nevada National Security Site (formerly known as Nevada Test Site, NTS)
properties.label: Frenchman Flat, Areas 5, 11
properties.Notes: Frenchman Flat was the first area at the NTS used, mostly for drop tests. A small city of structures, a pine forest and multiple cars, trucks and military vehicles were positioned on the salt flat for tests. It is the original "Doom Town".

properties.Site location: Nevada National Security Site (formerly known as Nevada Test Site, NTS)
properties.label: Yucca Flat, Areas 1–4, 6-10
properties.Notes: The heaviest used test area in the NTS, used early for atmospheric, tower, balloon and crater tests, later for bore-hole tests. The latter are responsible for the large number of subsidence craters for which the area is known.

properties.Site location: Nevada National Security Site (formerly known as Nevada Test Site, NTS)
properties.label: Area 13
properties.Notes: An area of the NTS which was used for only one zero-yield dispersion test. The area was returned to the Nellis Range, and the subject of considerable cleanup efforts for dispersed plutonium.

properties.Site location: Nevada National Security Site (formerly known as Nevada Test Site, NTS)
properties.label: Rainier Mesa, Area 12
properties.Notes: The area in which the butte terrain is very amenable for underground horizontal tunnel testing. Contains over a dozen large tunnel complexes and a couple of bore-hole tests.

properties.Site location: Nevada National Security Site (formerly known as Nevada Test Site, NTS)
properties.label: Pahute Mesa, Area 19-20
properties.Notes: The farthest north of the NTS areas, this area was used mainly for bore-hole testing in hard rock and hard cap rock situations. It hosted at least one hard rock cratering blast.

properties.Site location: Nevada National Security Site (formerly known as Nevada Test Site, NTS)
properties.label: Jackass Flat, Area 26
properties.Notes: A large area in the NTS southwest. It was not used for nuclear testing, but contains the Yucca Mountain nuclear waste repository, the MX missile mobile test site, the NERVA nuclear rocket test facilities, the BREN Tower, and the X tunnel facility in which depleted uranium weapons were tested. Many of these facilities have been torn down and removed.

properties.Site location: Nevada National Security Site (formerly known as Nevada Test Site, NTS)
properties.label: Dome Mountain, Area 30
properties.Notes: A mountainous area used for only one Operation Plowshare test, Buggy, in which five bombs in a row were fired in salvo, as a test of trench cutting using nukes.

properties.Site location: Nevada National Security Site (formerly known as Nevada Test Site, NTS)
properties.label: Area 15
properties.Notes: An area in the NTS in which three tests were executed. It is the site of the EPA's dairy farm which measured cow uptake of fission products, and the old Climax silver mine. Adjacent to the infamous Area 51, on its east edge.

properties.Site location: Nevada National Security Site (formerly known as Nevada Test Site, NTS)
properties.label: Shoshone Mountain, Area 16
properties.Notes: An area containing a single horizontal tunnel complex used for six separate nuclear tests. It is also the site for the Divine Strake conventional blast experiment, which was aborted by public protests.

properties.Site location: Nevada National Security Site (formerly known as Nevada Test Site, NTS)
properties.label: Tonopah Test Range (aka Area 52)
properties.Notes: The area owned by the Air Force as the Tonopah Test Range, in which conventional bombs and tactics are evaluated. In 1963, four nuclear dispersion tests in the Roller Coaster series were executed on Tonopah Range land.

properties.label: South Atlantic Ocean
properties.Notes: An area of the South Atlantic Ocean was used in the Argus tests, three rocket launches with small devices to explode in the ionosphere to test electron injection into the magnetosphere as a defensive weapon against enemy ICBM command and control.

properties.label: Central Nevada
properties.Notes: A site for the underground testing of large (5 Mt) weapons was sought, since the NTS alluvial valley wasn't suitable, by testing with a 1 Mt "calibration" weapon. The Faultless shot showed central Nevada to be too unstable. The search then turned to Amchitka Island in Alaska.

properties.label: Amchitka Island
properties.Notes: Amchitka Island in the Aleutian Islands chain was an alternate site for the underground testing of large nuclear warheads. Used for the Vela Uniform test Long Shot, it was tested for the larger role when Faultless in central Nevada proved that area unsuitable. The Millrow test showed suitability, and the island was used for the 5 Mt Cannikin test, the largest underground test ever performed.

properties.label: Carlsbad, New Mexico
properties.Notes: The site of the "Gnome" Plowshare project. It is 13.5 km (8.4 mi) southwest of the Waste Isolation Pilot Plant (WIPP) near Carlsbad.

properties.label: Farmington, New Mexico
properties.Notes: A site near Farmington, New Mexico hosted the Gas Buggy shot, an attempt to frac for natural gas.

properties.label: Parachute, Colorado
properties.Notes: A site near Rifle, Colorado was used to explode three nuclear devices simultaneously, hoping to create a reservoir in which oil and natural gas could collect. The test was named Rio Blanco.

properties.label: Rifle, Colorado
properties.Notes: The Rulison test was an attempt to frac shale and release gas and oil.

properties.label: Lawrence Livermore National Lab, CA
properties.Notes: The US's second national nuclear laboratory, LLNL designed weapons and did explosive, sub-critical testing in the hills west of the lab buildings.

properties.label: Salmon Site, Lumberton, Mississippi
properties.Notes: An underground salt dome site which was used for two separate Vela Uniform project tests.

properties.label: Rainier Mesa
properties.Notes: One of the tunnels in the Rainier Mesa complex was used to fire a verification test.

properties.label: Fallon, Nevada
properties.Notes: A Vela shot, named Shoal, was fired in a bore hole near Fallon, Nevada.

properties.label: Balapan
properties.Notes: The US mission to Kazakhstan set off a series of explosions in unused bore holes for Comprehensive Nuclear Test Ban Treaty calibration.

properties.label: Degelen (Omega)
properties.Notes: The US mission to Kazakhstan set off a series of explosions in an unused tunnel for Comprehensive Nuclear Test Ban Treaty calibration, and as test precursors for the eventual destruction of all tunnels in Degelen, known as the Omega series.

properties.label: Tech Area 49
properties.Notes: The US's first national laboratory, Los Alamos was created secretly during World War II to build the first nuclear weapon. During the 1958 moratorium on nuclear testing, a number of sub-critical tests were performed underground to learn more about the dynamics of explosions and the metallurgy of plutonium.The US's first nuclear weapons lab, founded in the Manhattan Project in high secrecy. Tech Area 49 is an open area south of the lab, where zero-yield tests were executed in shallow bore holes during the 1958 moratorium.

properties.label: Semipalatinsk Test Site, Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: The first Soviet nuclear test ground, used for all kinds of atmospheric and underground testing, as well as sub-critical tests.

properties.label: Ground Zero
properties.Notes: The first area of the Semipalatinsk Test Site to be used. Tower, ground and air dropped weapons were tested there.

properties.label: Sary Uzen/Murzhik
properties.Notes: An area used to test a score or so bore-hole emplaced weapons. Some zero-yield testing was done there as well.

properties.label: Degelen
properties.Notes: A mountain massif in the Kazakh plains, Degelen was bored with a hundred or so test tunnels. It was the site for the majority of the zero-yield testing at Semipalatinsk. After being returned to Kazakhstan, the tunnels were closed, then more permanently closed to discourage metal thievery.

properties.label: Balapan
properties.Notes: Balapan was the site of most of the Soviet bore-hole tests. It was also the site of the Chagan massive cratering test, which created Lake Chagan.

properties.label: A: Chyornaya Guba (Black Bay)
properties.Notes: The southern end of Novaya Zemlya has been the location for a number of underwater, tower and rocket drop tests of nuclear weapons. Rogachevo Air Base is also located in the area, the launch area for several of nuclear armed missiles targeted north on Sukhoy Nos.

properties.label: B: Matochin Shar (Matochin Strait)
properties.Notes: A mountainous area (on the south side of the north end of the strait which names the test area) is used for horizontal tunnel testing and zero-yield testing. It is still in use for the latter today.

properties.label: C: Sukhoy Nos (Dry Nose)
properties.Notes: The area on the north side of Matochin Strait, used for air and rocket tests of massive blasts. The Tsar Bomba was tested here. The name refers to the land that forms the peninsula on the north side of the west end of the strait.

properties.label: Kapustin Yar
properties.Notes: The first Soviet missile launch and test area. From the original V-2 rocket launch area almost a dozen nuclear armed missiles were launched to explode over Sary Sagan, Central and West Kazakhstan and the Volgograd Oblast (region in Russia).

properties.label: Kola Launch Area
properties.Notes: A launch area in the Barents Sea between the Kola peninsula and Novaya Zemlya.

properties.label: Orenburg, Russia
properties.Notes: The site of a Soviet Army exercise which included a live nuclear blast.

properties.label: Karagandy, Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: Karagandy had four of the Project K nuclear rockets explode in space above its soil.

properties.label: Kyzylorda, Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: Kyzylorda was the location over which the 5th Project K rocket propelled bomb exploded. It also hosts the Baikonur Cosmodrome, uninvolved in nuclear testing.

properties.label: West Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: West Kazakhstan hosted at least three high altitude detonations, USSR #s 82 and 83, and ZUR-215, all on rockets from Kapustin Yar.

properties.label: Komi, Russia
properties.Notes: This area contains the Vorkuta Sovietski Airbase, from which the two Rosa nuclear rockets were launched toward Sukhoy Nos.

properties.label: Volgograd, Russia
properties.Notes: The military Grom (Thunder) and Groza (Storm) atomic tests, on rockets from Kapustin Yar, occurred above this area.

properties.label: Arkhangelsk, Russia
properties.Notes: This region contains Novaya Zemlya, but besides that it hosts three seismic probe PNEs, Kvartz 1 (Quartz), Rubin 1 (Ruby), and Globus 2 (Globe).

properties.label: Astrakhan, Russia
properties.Notes: This region has the Kapustin Yar space center, from which many nuclear armed missiles were tested. 15 tests to create natural gas reservoirs, called the Vega series are in Astrakhan.

properties.label: Bashkortostan, Russia
properties.Notes: This autonomous republic contains six PNEs, five named Butan (Butane) and concerned with oil/gas recovery intensification, and two Kama for creating chemical waste storage areas.

properties.label: Irkutsk, Russia
properties.Notes: Hosts the seismic probe PNEs Meteorit 4 (Meteorite) and Rift 3.

properties.label: Ivanovo, Russia
properties.Notes: Hosts the Globus 1 seismic probe PNE.

properties.label: Kemerovo, Russia
properties.Notes: Kemorovo contains a single seismic probe PNE: Kvartz 4 (Quartz).

properties.label: Khanty-Mansi, Russia
properties.Notes: This district hosts five different PNEs: Kraton 1 (Craton), Kvarts 3 (Quartz) and Kimberlit 1 (Kimberlite), seismic probes; and Angara and Benzol (Benzine), oil intensifications.

properties.label: Komi, Russia
properties.Notes: This region contains PNEs Globus 3 and 4 (Globe), Kvarts 2 (Quartz), Gorizont (Horizon), all seismic probes.

properties.label: Krasnoyarsk, Russia
properties.Notes: The area has seven seismic probe PNEs: Meteorit 2 and 3 (Meteorite), Kimberlit 3 (Kimberlite), Batholit 1 (Batholith), Kraton 2 (Craton), 'Gorizont 3 (Horizon) and Rift 4, and an oil intensification PNE: Schpat 2 (Spar).

properties.label: Kalmykia, Russia
properties.Notes: The Republic of Kalmykia hosts the Region 4 seismic probe PNE.

properties.label: Murmansk, Russia
properties.Notes: Murmansk holds two apatite recovery PNEs: Dnepr 1 and 2.

properties.label: Nenetsky, Russia
properties.Notes: Holds one seismic probe PNE: Pirit 1 (Pyrite), a failed attempt to close a burning gas well.

properties.label: Orenburg, Russia
properties.Notes: Besides the infamous Totskoye nuclear exercise, Orenburg hosts three seismic probe PNEs: Magistral (Highway) and Region 1 and 2, and an oil intensification PNE: Sapfir 1 and 2 (Sapphire).

properties.label: Perm, Russia
properties.Notes: Perm hosts three channel digging PNEs: Tiaga 1,2 and 3, and seven oil intensification PNEs: Geligy 1-5 (Helium) and Grifon 1-2 (Griffin).

properties.label: Sakha, Russia
properties.Notes: Hosts seismic probe PNEs Kimberlit 4 (Kimberlite), Kraton 3-4 (Craton), an excavation PNE Krystal (Crystal), and oil intensification shots of the Neva series of 5.

properties.label: Stavropol, Russia
properties.Notes: Stavropol contains a single PNE named Stavropol, for gas intensification.

properties.label: Tyumen, Russia
properties.Notes: Tyumen contains a single PNE Tavda, designed for underground gas storage.

properties.label: Yamalo-Nenets, Russia
properties.Notes: Contains the three seismic probe PNEs: Gorizont 2 (Horizon), Rift 1 and Rubin 1 (Ruby).

properties.label: Zabaykalsky, Russia
properties.Notes: The long range atomic missile test Tyulpan (Tulip) originated from this district, then known as Chita.

properties.label: Aktobe, Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: The site of a single seismic sounding PNE named Basolit 2 (Batholith).

properties.label: Atyrau, Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: The location for a series of PNEs exploring the use of salt domes for storage of natural gas, name Galit (Halite or rock salt).

properties.label: Karagandy, Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: Containing part of Semipalatinsk and all of Sary Shagan, Karagandy has a single PNE named Meridian 1, but also had four of the Project K nuclear rockets explode in space above its soil.

properties.label: Kostanay, Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: Kostanay Province hosted a single PNE called Region 5, one of the seismic probing series.

properties.label: Mangystau, Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: Mangystau hosted three shallow PNEs known as Say Utes. Their purpose may have been exploration for a high energy weapons test site.

properties.label: South Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: South Kazakhstan hosted a single PNE named Meridian 3, one of the seismic probe shots.

properties.label: West Kazakhstan
properties.Notes: West Kazakhstan hosted three underground cavity experiments named Lira, and Region 2, a seismic probe. There were also military high altitude tests.

properties.label: Bukhara, Uzbekistan
properties.Notes: Near Turkmenistan, another gas well fire was extinguished with a nuke named Urta-Bulak.

properties.label: Kashkadarya, Uzbekistan
properties.Notes: Kashkadarya hosted a single PNE call Pamuk, to successfully extinguish a gas well fire.

properties.label: Donetsk, Ukraine
properties.Notes: The Donetsk region of Ukraine hosted a single PNE name Klivazh (Cleavage), a shot designed to releieve gas pressure in a coal deposit.

properties.label: Kharkiv, Ukraine
properties.Notes: Kharkiv hosted a single PNE named Fakel (Torch), an unsuccessful effort to extinguish a burning gas well.

properties.label: Montebello Islands, Western Australia, Australia
properties.Notes: An archipelago of islands on Australia's northwest coast, the area was chosen for three British tests. The first, Hurricane, was in a ship in a waterway; the other two were tower shots on a couple of the islands.

properties.label: Woomera Prohibited Area
properties.Notes: The site of a pair of British tower tests named Totem. Test site is no longer part of the Woomera Prohibited Area.

properties.label: Woomera Prohibited Area
properties.Notes: The site of a pair of British test series named Buffalo and Antler, as well as a long series of "small" tests: safety tests, zero yield tests and so on. Test site is no longer part of the Woomera Prohibited Area.

properties.label: Kiritimati, Kiribati
properties.Notes: Formerly known as Christmas Island (the Pacific one, not the one in the Indian Ocean) Kiritimati was used as an air base for bombers dropping nuclear tests mostly south of the island It hosted most of the Operation Dominic drops as well as most of Britain's Operation Grapple.

properties.label: Malden Island, Kiribati
properties.Notes: Three of the UK's Operation Grapple series were exploded above this island.

properties.label: Nevada Test Site
properties.Notes: In 1958 the US and the UK concluded the 1958 US–UK Mutual Defence Agreement. Among other things, it gave the UK the right to use the US testing facilities to test their weapon designs. The UK tested 24 weapons underground in the US starting in 1962, ending when the Comprehensive Nuclear Test Ban Treaty closed all testing in 1991.

properties.label: Reggane (CEMO)
properties.Notes: A French experimental facility, Centre Saharien d'Expérimentations Militaires (CEMO), was chosen for France's initial bomb tests. The fourth bomb Gerboise Verte had to be scuttled so it would not be confiscated by the Algerian regime in revolt.

properties.label: In Ekker (CSEM)
properties.Notes: A large mountain massif in Algeria near the village of In Eker was leased from Algeria for French underground testing base under the name Centre d'Expérimentations Militaires des Oasis (CSEM); the nearby smaller rock of Adrar Tikertine was the site of five safety tests named Pollen.

properties.label: Moruroa Atoll
properties.Notes: The primary atoll for French nuclear tests in French Polynesia. Earlier tests were air drops in the area, later bore holes into the coral and volcanic rock underneath were used.

properties.label: Fangataufa Atoll
properties.Notes: The secondary atoll for French tests, used when Muroroa became too contaminated for crew safety.

properties.label: Area A: Nanshan
properties.Notes: The Nanshan (South Mountain) featured a mountain bluff ideal for horizontal tunneling. Four, and later a fifth, tunnels were bored to accommodate six nuclear tests and a series of sub-critical tests.

properties.label: Area B: Qinggir
properties.Notes: Qinggir is an area of broken, but fairly level ground which was exploited for 13 boreholes used for underground tests.

properties.label: Area C: Beishan
properties.Notes: Beishan (North Mountain) was used for tunnels for two tests.

properties.label: Area D: Drop Area
properties.Notes: An area of Lop Nur set aside for bomb drops from airplanes, and was the site of 596, the first Chinese (tower) explosion.

properties.label: Jiuquan Satellite Launch Center
properties.Notes: China's first civil and military space operations base, the nuclear launch was probably from Site 2 or 3, now inactive.

properties.label: Pokhran
properties.Notes: An area in India's western desert. The first test was in an open area, the rest in a containment several miles southwest.

properties.label: Kirana Hills
properties.Notes: A series of low hills in a populated area in Pakistan, in which the Army dug tunnels in which to perform "cold" (zero-yield) tests in preparation for building their nuclear weapon.

properties.label: Ras Koh
properties.Notes: The test site for the first Pakistani weapon, a horizontal tunnel dug under a mountain massif. The trademark pictures of the test are of the mountain shaking off a layer of dust and avalanching rock from the internal impact.

properties.label: Kharan Desert
properties.Notes: The site for the balance of the Pakistani nuclear testing, a single test which may have had from 1 to 6 weapons included, all fired simultaneously.

properties.label: Punggye-ri Test Site
properties.Notes: A test area for tunnel tests in northeastern North Korea. It sported several horizontal tunnels under the surrounding mountains but eventually testing settled on a single area under a mountain to the north of the base. The 2017 test weakened the mountain support and caused a cave-in, resulting in a reported 200 worker deaths. Punggye-ri was formally closed in May 2018, and destruction of the facilities and the tunnels was witnessed.

properties.label: Vastrap Field
properties.Notes: The intended site for South Africa's first nuclear tests. The site was prepared for use, and then abandoned when the apartheid government decided to give up nuclear weapons.

properties.label: Campo de Provas Brigadeiro Velloso
properties.Notes: Brazil had a semi-clandestine weapons program called the "Parallel Program" until 1990 when President Fernando Collor de Mello canceled it, and this was to be the test site.
